## Introduction


In the study of economics, supply refers to the quantity of a good or service that producers are willing and able to offer for sale at different prices over a specific period. This concept is crucial for understanding market dynamics and the equilibrium between supply and demand.

## 2.2.1 Supply, Price & Quantity


### The Supply Curve


The supply curve is a graphical representation that shows the relationship between the price of a good and the quantity supplied by producers. It typically slopes upward from left to right, indicating a positive relationship between price and quantity supplied.

* **Upward Slope**: This upward slope means that as the price increases, the quantity supplied also increases. Conversely, a decrease in price leads to a decrease in the quantity supplied.
* **Rational Producers**: Rational, profit-maximizing producers are incentivized to supply more at higher prices to maximize their profits.

### Law of Supply


The law of supply states that there is a direct relationship between the price of a good and the quantity supplied, ceteris paribus (all other factors being constant).

* **Price Increase**: When the price rises, the quantity supplied increases.
* **Price Decrease**: When the price falls, the quantity supplied decreases.

<callout type="example">If the price of apples increases from $2 to $3 per kilogram, an apple farmer might increase their supply from 100 kilograms to 150 kilograms to take advantage of the higher price.</callout>

### Individual and Market Supply


* **Individual Supply**: This refers to the supply of a good or service by a single producer.
* **Market Supply**: This is the aggregate of all individual supplies of a good or service at each price level. It is calculated by summing the individual supplies at each price.

<callout type="example">Suppose there are four bakeries in a small town. The market supply of bread is the sum of the quantities each bakery is willing to supply at a given price. If Bakery 1 supplies 300 loaves, Bakery 2 supplies 600 loaves, Bakery 3 supplies 180 loaves, and Bakery 4 supplies 320 loaves, the market supply is 1400 loaves.</callout>

### Graphical Representation


_Caption: A supply curve showing an upward slope, indicating the positive relationship between price and quantity supplied._

__

![The supply curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/dd53dcaf9fd6c52b23c0eea988da2ac6a3396fdc-1120x846.png)

## 2.2.2 Non-Price Determinants of Supply


Several factors can shift the entire supply curve, changing the quantity supplied at every price level. These are known as non-price determinants of supply.

### Changes to Costs of Production


* **Increase in Costs**: If the price of raw materials or other inputs increases, the cost of production rises, leading firms to supply less at each price level. This shifts the supply curve to the left.
* **Decrease in Costs**: Conversely, if costs decrease, firms can supply more at each price level, shifting the supply curve to the right.

$$ \text{COP Increases} \Rightarrow S \text{ Decreases (Shifts Left)} $$

$$ \text{COP Decreases} \Rightarrow S \text{ Increases (Shifts Right)} $$

### Indirect Taxes and Subsidies


* **Indirect Taxes**: An increase in taxes increases production costs, shifting the supply curve to the left. A decrease in taxes shifts it to the right.
* **Subsidies**: Government subsidies lower production costs, shifting the supply curve to the right. Removing subsidies shifts it to the left.

$$ \text{Taxes Increase} \Rightarrow S \text{ Decreases (Shifts Left)} $$

$$ \text{Subsidy Increases} \Rightarrow S \text{ Increases (Shifts Right)} $$

<callout type="warning">A common error is to think that a subsidy shifts the demand curve. In reality, it shifts the supply curve to the right, leading to a lower equilibrium price and higher quantity.</callout>

### Technological Changes


* **New Technology**: Advances in technology increase productivity and lower production costs, shifting the supply curve to the right.
* **Ageing Technology**: Deteriorating technology can reduce productivity, shifting the supply curve to the left.

$$ \text{Technology Increases} \Rightarrow S \text{ Increases (Shifts Right)} $$

$$ \text{Technology Decreases} \Rightarrow S \text{ Decreases (Shifts Left)} $$

### Number of Firms


* **Increase in Firms**: More firms entering the market increase total supply, shifting the supply curve to the right.
* **Decrease in Firms**: Fewer firms reduce total supply, shifting the supply curve to the left.

$$ \text{No. of Firms Increases} \Rightarrow S \text{ Increases (Shifts Right)} $$

$$ \text{No. of Firms Decreases} \Rightarrow S \text{ Decreases (Shifts Left)} $$

### Weather Events


* **Adverse Weather**: Events like droughts or floods can decrease supply, shifting the supply curve to the left.
* **Favorable Weather**: Good weather conditions can increase supply, shifting the supply curve to the right.

$$ \text{Drought} \Rightarrow S \text{ Decreases (Shifts Left)} $$

$$ \text{Good Weather} \Rightarrow S \text{ Increases (Shifts Right)} $$

### Future Price Expectations


* **Price Increase Expectation**: If firms expect prices to rise in the future, they may increase current supply, shifting the supply curve to the right.
* **Price Decrease Expectation**: If firms expect prices to fall, they may reduce current supply, shifting the supply curve to the left.

$$ \text{Expectations Price Will Rise} \Rightarrow S \text{ Increases (Shifts Right)} $$

$$ \text{Expectations Price Will Fall} \Rightarrow S \text{ Decreases (Shifts Left)} $$

### Goods in Joint and Competitive Supply


* **Joint Supply**: When the production of one good also produces another (e.g., beef and leather), an increase in the supply of one will increase the supply of the other.
* **Competitive Supply**: When goods compete for the same resources (e.g., wheat and potatoes), an increase in the supply of one will decrease the supply of the other.

$$ \text{Supply of Good A Increases} \Rightarrow S \text{ of Good B Increases (Shifts Right)} $$

$$ \text{Supply of Good A Increases} \Rightarrow S \text{ of Good B Decreases (Shifts Left)} $$

## Conclusion


Understanding the supply side of the market is essential for comprehending how prices are determined and how markets function. The supply curve illustrates the relationship between price and quantity supplied, while the non-price determinants of supply explain shifts in the supply curve. By analyzing these factors, economists can better predict market behaviors and outcomes.
